24.1.label: Question 24.1
24.1.scenario:
A leading life insurer is considering broadening its individual product range. It is considering

launching income protection (IP) insurance, critical illness (CI) insurance, private medical
insurance (PMI) and long‐term care insurance (LTCI) products. All contracts will be available only
on an individual basis.
24.1.question: Describe the main product specific risks associated with each of these contracts.

Overall risks: The primary risk for all products is that future experience turns out more adverse (and hence
more expensive for the insurer) than assumed in the pricing basis.                            [½]
For the long\u2010term products (IP insu:           anti‐selection risk at entry, as high risk individuals choose to take out insurance           [½]
          selective withdrawal risk, as only healthy policyholders tend to lapse their policies         [½]
          early withdrawal risk (when the asset share negative), so an overall loss will be
IP insurance:           expense risk – expenses, especially claims management, could be much higher than allowed
for in the pricing basis, and can be hard to control                                  [½]
          policy wording risk – eg imprecise claim definitions, allowing more claims to occur and
allowing claims to continue for longer durations than expected                       [½]
CI insurance:           rates of illness diagnosis higher than assumed ...                                            [½]
... for example, improved medical screening leads to earlier diagnosis, earlier claims and
          policy‐wording risk, eg imprecise CI definitions, allowing claims to be accepted for
## PMI: ... which relates to the frequency of referral for treatment by doctors                       [½]
          claim amounts, ie the cost of treatments, greater than assumed ...                             [¼]
... over which the insurer may have little control                                            [½]
          the risk from a few very large claims occurring, ...                                          [½]
... or from an aggregation of a large number of claims occurring together, eg because of a
         the risk of non‐disclosure of pre‐existing conditions, whether at outset or at time of claim
         volume of business – there will be a significant risk from inadequate volumes (low sales or
...and these may be influenced by changes in State provision ...                                  [½]
## LTCI:          rates of transfer from healthy to sick states and between increasingly higher benefit sick
states (risk that they are higher than expected)                                       [½]
         capital strain risk may be significant as reserves and/or solvency capital requirements will
–         high levels of uncertainty surrounding the assumptions                                  [½]
–         the extent of the guarantees offered                                                    [½]
         investment risk as the product generally requires large reserves                                  [½]
         reputational / marketing risk because LTCI policies often do not meet 100% of the care costs
(eg because a monetary maximum benefit is stipulated in the policy), whereas
policyholders may believe themselves to be fully covered on an indemnity basis           [½]
         expense risk, eg through high claims management and underwriting costs                     [½]

24.2.label: Question 24.2
24.2.scenario:
A health and care insurer offers a without‐profits pre‐funded long‐term care insurance contract.

Under this contract the regular premium and the amount of monthly benefit are both indexed
annually in line with a national index of retail prices. The indexation of benefit applies both
before and after a claim starts.

Premiums are payable until a claim starts or until age 75, whichever is sooner.

The contract gives four different levels of benefit, based on different amounts of care needed.
The lowest level provides for a minimum amount of home care while the highest level provides
for round‐the‐clock residential care. The benefits for the four levels of care are set initially at
fixed sums per month, sufficient at current prices to pay for the amounts of care required. The
need for care will be assessed independently.
24.2.question: Discuss the risks involved for the insurer in writing this contract.

Solution: Higher prices give more potential profit per policy, meeting Point (4), but if this reduces
marketability, it can adversely affect business volumes, thereby reducing the overall return on
capital.
6.         Profitability: What is the expected profit, and its variance, from the existing
business?
The process of checking the profitability of in‐force business is not very different from that
for new business, which was covered earlier in the course.
It is important that an insurance company does not stop considering the profitability of its
business once it is written, but continues to monitor it as part of its control cycle. If the
business turns out to be unprofitable, then the insurance company will know that it
should be taking action, such as:
Certain product designs can produce a lot of capital strain when they are sold. If such a product is
marketable, leading to large volumes of new business, then the insurance company will need a
large supply of capital to prevent supervisory insolvency.
10.        Risk management: How can underwriting and reinsurance be used to manage risk
so that the company can increase profits, whilst keeping its risk profile within the
resources available to it?
Underwriting and reinsurance are two key tools of risk control for a health and care
insurance company. These areas shall be considered fully in the next two chapters.
Both activities cost money, so the more carefully you underwrite policies / the more reinsurance
cover you take out, the less your expected profit will be, all other things being equal.
11.        Claims: Are the claims procedures adequate? Are the claims functions being
properly followed? Are there effective fraud control measures in place?
This risk was covered in the previous two chapters. Remember that with health and care
insurance, the use of third parties increases the risk of claims deterioration, particularly
without adequate procedures and controls.
Tension with Point (5) – Increasing security by increasing reserves (or solvency capital) increases
the capital needed to finance new policies, reducing the return on capital produced and thereby
making the policies more expensive. Passing this cost to the policyholder by increasing premiums
or charges could reduce marketability, causing further reduction in returns.
Tension with Point (6) – Increasing reserves (or solvency capital) will cause a deferral of all
remaining profit from existing business, thereby reducing its present value to the providers of
capital.
Tension with Point (8) – Unless a compensating capital injection is made, increasing reserves (or
solvency capital) will reduce the free assets (working capital) available to the insurance company,
which may reduce investment freedom.
Tension with Point (9) – Increasing reserves (or solvency capital) reduces the capital available.
This can affect the insurance company's ability to develop and sell business in the volumes it
would like, or to follow a particular approach to smoothing profit distribution over time.
Claim transition probabilities: There is a risk of underestimating the rates at which people will start a claim …                           [½]
… and also the rates at which they move to higher levels of need and therefore of benefit level.[½]
These probabilities are particularly difficult to estimate if reliable data are scarce.                     [½]
A further element of unpredictability is the independent assessment of need, ...                            [½]
... in particular the interpretation of policy literature by the assessor / policyholder.                   [½]
Longevity: The insurer is at risk of underestimating how long people will live.                                        [½]
Expenses and expense inflation: The insurer is at risk of losing money if expenses are higher than expected …                               [½]
… notably through higher‐than‐expected inflation of some/all expenses.                                      [½]
The insurer is at risk of not covering its fixed expenses. This will happen if new business volumes
Benefit inflation: Benefits may increase more than expected because of high inflation.                                    [½]
Although premiums are also indexed, this would not be sufficient to cover the increased cost of
benefits because the whole of the benefit will be increased but not all the premiums would be.
In particular, unexpectedly high inflation at long durations in force (ie shortly before a claim)
might significantly increase the benefit with only a small effect on total premium income …            [½]
… although this might be offset by higher nominal investment returns.                                  [½]
Subject to the last point, the insurer is even more vulnerable to inflation after premiums have
ceased, either for a claim or due to reaching age 75.                                           [½]
There may also be a marketing risk if care costs (which will be to some extent earnings‐linked)
inflate at rates in excess of retail price inflation.                                           [½]
The benefit might not then cover the care needed, leading to dissatisfied customers and bad
Investment return: Premiums may have to be invested over a very long period and substantial funds may build up in
Investment return is therefore a significant risk.                                                     [½]
The indexed benefit means that the insurer will need to seek a return that at least matches
Investment in index‐linked bonds would reduce the risk, …                                              [½]
… but the insurer may also choose to invest in equities, particularly if index‐linked securities are
Equity investment represents a higher risk, but this may not be perceived as a serious problem for
Persistency: Whenever the asset share is negative, there is a financial risk from withdrawal.                       [½]
At other times, the extent of such a risk depends on how much any surrender benefit paid
There is also a risk that selective withdrawals will lead to a less healthy insured population than
Capital strain: Because this is a regular premium without‐profits contract, with significant guarantees, this
product will need high reserves (and solvency capital) and hence will cause capital strain at entry.
So the insurer is at risk from new business strain if it sells higher volumes than expected.    [½]
Syllabus objective: 3.1        Assess how the following can be a source of risk to a health and care insurance
0 Introduction: The last two chapters covered the various sources of risk to a health and care insurer. This
chapter outlines the problems that the existence of these risks may create.
The actuary plays a key role in helping the insurer to deal with its problems, and this chapter
briefly considers the key areas of decision making and concerns with which the actuary is
significantly involved. However, remember that these are problems that confront the insurer as a
whole, not just the actuary, and all of the insurer’s management needs to be party both to the
1 The aims of the company and of the a: The management of an insurance company will have several aims. Of most relevance here,
          maximise (or optimise within an acceptable level of risk) the profits of the company,
whether these go to shareholders or to mutual policyholders
          maximise (or optimise within an acceptable level of risk) the return that the company
Usually these will go together, but the Board will want to be sure that the capital in the
company is being used to the best advantage, particularly where it needs to decide between
a number of competing uses of that capital.
Chapter 19 covered the relative merits of aiming to maximise the present value of profit or the
percentage return on capital. While these will usually lead to the same conclusions and business
Note that the phrase ‘mutual policyholders’ relates to mutual insurers where all profits
ultimately belong to the with-profits policyholders and the company is managed on their
The other extreme is the proprietary company where all profits belong to shareholders, who
own the equity of the organisation. There are other concerns where profits (possibly
deriving from certain sources) are divided in specified proportions between the
\uf0b7 the resources available:           the public interest need, as usually expressed in insurance supervisory legislation,
for the company to avoid insolvency
\uf0b7 the requirements of othe: It would clearly be disastrous for a company to be declared insolvent by the regulators. This is a
potential consequence of risk with which an actuary must be particularly concerned when giving
advice to the insurer. Note that while supervisory insolvency is a critical business risk for the
insurer, the policyholders will almost certainly receive most or all of the fair value of their policies,
depending on the particular criteria that are applied by the supervisors to define insolvency.
Whether the policies can continue in force, perhaps administered by a different insurer, will
depend on the circumstances of the case.
2 Implications for the insurance compa: This section covers the various aspects of the insurance company’s strategic decision‐making
processes that depend on risk, and that therefore need actuarial attention.
The problems that will then confront act: 1.        Policy data: Are the data provided by the company complete and accurate?
If the data are so inaccurate as to lead the actuary to give incorrect advice then this is
potentially very serious, both for the insurance company and the actuary. Therefore the
actuary would normally carry out some checks on the quality of the data. It is imperative
that the actuary is at least aware of any data problems.
Chapter 27 covers the checks that might be made on policy and claims data.
2.        Product design: What contracts should the company offer and what benefits and
design features should be included in them, given its risk profile and the resources
Chapters 6 and 7 of the course introduced factors that an actuary will consider when
designing a product. In particular, they identified a number of factors that should be
considered when assessing the suitability of a proposed product design.
Question: If you had to choose one word to describe the overall approach to be used in a supervisory
valuation, what would it be?
Explain how a product that is marketable and profitable, and provides a good return on capital,
can cause capital management problems for a company.
Suggest briefly any ways in which increasing the level of underwriting and reinsurance might be
counter‐productive to the insurance company’s aims.
Describe the tensions that exist between Point (7) and each of Points (5), (6), (8) and (9) above.
\uf0b7 the onerousness of any gu:          the sensitivity of profit to variations in future experience
\uf0b7 regulatory constraints an: 3.         Product marketability: How can the company optimise the balance between price
and volumes of business (both new business and renewing business for short-term
insurance contracts), taking into account competition?
There is an obvious relationship between price and volumes of business: high (low) prices
will tend to lead to low (high) volumes of business, however, this will depend to some
extent on the elasticity of demand for the product. The elasticity of demand for the
product will be strongly affected by the level of competition in the market.
In order to change the volume of business sold, as well as changing the price charged for
the product, the actuary might consider adding features to improve its marketability, for
example, options and guarantees. This must all be balanced in order to optimise the total
revenues and hence profits to the insurance company.
4.         Pricing: What is the expected profit, and its variance, from selling a new contract at
particular premium rates or with particular charges and will the company have the
resources to sell the contract on those terms?
The emphasis here is to find a price that makes selling the policy worthwhile to the
insurance company, and will not over‐stretch its capital as a result.
5.         Return on capital: What return on capital will the company expect to make by
investing its capital in the development and issue of a new insurance contract?
Here the issue is more to do with the overall decision to develop and launch particular
products at particular prices. So, while from (3) a product might be potentially profitable
and within the insurance company’s capital resources, the volume of business may not
produce sufficient reward (return) for the amount of capital invested in the project.
These last two closely‐related ideas are central to an insurance company’s business aims.
\uf0b7 altering withdrawal payme:          altering the expense, sickness or mortality charges, if possible
\uf0b7 reducing actual renewal e:          ensuring that the contracts currently being sold are not likely to suffer the same
fate (ie they are not too sensitive to whatever has caused the losses on the
7.        Supervisory reserves: What assumptions should be used so that the reserves and
solvency capital requirements provide adequate security for policyholders?
The overall approach could be described as ‘prudent’, in that the purpose of holding reserves
together with the solvency capital is to ensure that the company has a high probability of paying
its liabilities in future. The higher this probability is, all else being equal, the greater the
For example, by increasing the VaR probability to 99.9% from 99.5%, the valuation would become
8.        Investment: How should the company invest its assets to maximise expected return
within the resources available to it?
A health and care insurance company may:          to meet any guaranteed benefits with a high degree of certainty
         to adopt an adventurous investment policy in pursuit of the best returns.
Its key resource in pursuing high returns is its free assets. These provide it with a cushion
in the event of adverse experience. The greater the free assets, the more adventurous
the investment policy can be, all other things being equal.
9.         Capital management: Will the company be able to achieve its short, medium and
long-term plans given the resources available to it?
In order to give itself a better idea of its long‐term capital position, an insurance company
can project the future expected values of its assets and liabilities. It can then investigate
whether its investment policy, new business plans and expense control plans represent a
